children.name: Bradypus
children.description: Three-toed sloths — the slowest mammals on Earth and the vertebrates with the lowest recorded metabolic rate for their body size. Sloths spend 15–20 hours per day hanging upside down and sleeping, descending to the forest floor just once a week to defecate — a slow, conspicuous, and dangerous process that accounts for a high proportion of all sloth deaths. Their organs are anchored to their ribs rather than the spine, so hanging inverted requires no muscular effort. A greenish alga grows in grooves in their fur, providing camouflage and perhaps supplementary nutrition; sloth fur also hosts a dedicated ecosystem of moths, beetles, and mites found nowhere else. Three-toed sloths cannot regulate their temperature well and depend on moving between sun and shade to thermoregulate.

children.name: Bradypus tridactylus
children.description: The largest three-toed sloth. Males have a distinctive orange-yellow patch on their back. Found in the rainforests of northeastern South America. Moved so rarely that green algae colonise their fur for camouflage.

children.name: Bradypus variegatus
children.description: The most widespread and common sloth, found from Honduras to Brazil. The species best studied for its unusual microbiome. Heart rate can drop to 40 bpm during rest. Swims well — faster in water than on land.

children.name: Bradypus torquatus
children.description: Vulnerable. Restricted to the Atlantic Forest of Brazil, one of the world's most threatened biomes. Distinguished by long black mane fur around the head and neck. Estimated fewer than 10,000 individuals remain.

children.name: Bradypus pygmaeus
children.description: Critically endangered. Found only on Isla Escudo de Veraguas, a tiny island off Panama. Approximately 40% smaller than mainland sloths — an example of island dwarfism. Fewer than 100 individuals may survive.
